texts.orig: As the world's population continues to increase, so too will the need for additional, cleaner sources of energy. Total worldwide energy consumption is expected to grow by over 50% by the year 2035 [1]. This trend, coupled with increasing governmental legislation promoting more ef fi cient energy technologies and lower CO2 emissions has placed natural gas in the spotlight. Due to its lower carbon emissions, as well as development of new technologies such as horizontal drilling, natural gas consumption is projected to increase from 2012 levels by 52% in the year 2035 [1]. Despite this promising outlook, almost all raw natural gas requires pretreatment before transmission to the consumer. While CH4 usually constitutes 70 -90% of a natural gas well [2,3], a number of other contaminants such as CO2, H2S and hydrocarbons can exist in gas wells. texts.label: text
texts.orig: A 6FDA based polyamide-imide, 6F-PAI-1, is compared to Pebax s , a commercially available rubbery polyether/polyamide block copolymer, for the simultaneous separation of CO2 and H2S from CH4. Feed streams of 20/20/60 and 5/45/50H2S/CO2/CH4 were used to compare the effect of acid gas concentration on the separation ef fi ciency of 6F-PAI-1 and Pebax s under industrially relevant conditions. 6F-PAI-1 showed CO2/CH4 selectivities at 850 psia total feed pressure of 30 and 40 for the 20/20/60 and 5/45/50 feed streams, respectively, while selectivity for H2S/CH4 was approximately 20 for both feeds. Pebax s showed selectivities of 40 and 10 for H2S/CH4 and CO2/CH4, respectively. Both selectivities were mostly independent of acid gas concentration in the feed, an unsurprising trend considering the non-glassy nature of this material. The selectivities in 6F-PAI-1 translated to less than 6% CH4 lost in the permeate stream for both feeds, while for the 5/45/50 feed, CH4 fraction in the permeate at 850 psia was less than 4%. These promising results suggest that glassy polymers possessing favorable intrinsic plasticization resistance, such as 6F-PAI-1, may be appropriate for the typical case of natural gas sweetening where CO2 concentration in the feed is higher than it is for H2S.

texts.orig: Gas separation using polymeric membranes offers a number of key advantages over traditional, thermally based technologies. Because the driving force for membrane separations relies upon the partial pressure difference of the gas species across the membrane, the naturally high pressure of the gas well acts as a source of ' free energy. ' Additionally, the mechanical and operational simplicity of membranes allow for easier installation and retro fi tting into existing equipment as well as eliminating the need for constant operational monitoring. Despite such advantages, the performance of pure polymeric materials can be severely compromised by plasticization in aggressive feeds. This occurs when high activity components such as CO2 and H2S swell the polymer chains, leading to signi fi cant decreases in separation ef fi ciency. Many of the past research efforts toward membrane based natural gas puri fi cation have targeted preventing such non-ideal effects [4 -10].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: membrane based natural gas puri fi cation has only considered CO2 as a contaminant due to the high toxicity of H2S. To safely handle H2S, additional and strict safety measures beyond that of CO2 are required. As such, the few literature studies addressing H2S removal using membranes have been limited to very low H2S concentration and feed pressure [11 -15]. Additionally, because of the difference in physical properties between H2S and CO2, such as their size and condensability relative to CH4, it is dif fi cult to optimize the selectivity of materials for both acid gases relative to CH4 without compromising the plasticization resistance of most materials [16]. Consequently, studies focusing on membrane based natural gas puri fi cation typically address these contaminants separately. Studies working on H2S removal have focused almost exclusively on rubbery polymers, which separate gases based on solubility differences and offer high H2S/CH4 selectivity but low CO2/CH4 selectivity. Conversely, glassy polymers, which separate gases mainly on their difference in size, offer much higher selectivities for CO2 over CH4 compared to H2S over CH4 and have been the dominant material applied to CO2 removal. Only a few studies have looked at the simultaneous removal of both contaminants and have only considered low pressures and acid gas concentrations [12,13,16], conditions which do not accurately re fl ect aggressive natural gas wells. One of the primary challenges in using membranes for natural gas puri fi cation stems from the fact that most natural gas wells often contain both acid gases at varying levels; however, the concentration of CO2 is almost always higher than that of H2S [2]. Therefore, a membrane unit with high CO2/CH4 selectivity and moderate H2S/CH4 selectivity, such as a glassy polymer, may be more suitable for this type of gas separation. Indeed, Hao et al. simulated different con fi gurations of H2S selective membranes, CO2 selective membranes, and both types of membranes in series. They concluded that a single membrane that is more selective towards the contaminant gas of highest feed concentration resulted in the lowest processing cost [11,12]. Also, Baker et al. suggested, following their extensive work on rubbery polymers for the separation of natural gas containing H2S and CO2 in the range of 0 -5% and 3 -20%, respectively, that rubbery polymers would be unable to reduce the CO2 concentration down to pipeline grade without vastly over processing the feed due to rubber's intrinsically low CO2/CH4 selectivity [14]. Because membranes alone, both rubbery or glassy, may not be able to bring acid gas down to pipeline speci fi cation without some form of additional processing, they may be used as a bulk separation unit, with a downstream H2S scavenging unit acting as the fi nal polishing step in order to bring the gas to pipeline grade. The type of membrane will depend on the process conditions. For a typical natural gas well where CO2 concentration is higher than H2S, the optimal material would have high CO2/CH4 selectivity in the presence of elevated concentrations of H2S, thereby providing an opportunity for the use of glassy polymers for the simultaneous separation of both CO2 and H2S. Additionally, the characterization of such materials at industrially relevant conditions, which is very dif fi cult due to the toxicity of H2S, is an important goal for both academic as well as industrial research. We have designed and built a state-of-the-art research facility with all the necessary safety precautions in place for safe study of H2S in both pure gas as well as industrially applicable mixed gas conditions. The details of such equipment are described in the paper by Kraftschik and Koros [18]. The goal of this paper is to compare rubbery and glassy polymers to enable simultaneous separation of both CO2 and H2S from CH4 at industrially relevant conditions. Additionally, the effect of pendent fl uorine in the polymer backbone on membrane stability against H2S induced plasticization will be discussed brie fl y.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our previous work highlighted a class of 6FDA based polyamide-imides, which have shown comparable CO2 plasticization resistance to crosslinked polyimides without the need for covalent crosslinking [17,18]. One such polyamide-imide, 6F-PAI-1, has shown enhanced stability against H2S induced swelling using pure gas feeds compared to other polyamide-imides, 6F-PAI-2 and 6FPAI-3, both of which bear lower fl uorine concentration along their backbones. The structures of these materials are shown in Fig. 1. This trend is believed to be due to reduced H2S solubility in the more highly fl uorinated media, a phenomenon which has been observed and well documented by Merkel et al. [20]. The fi rst section of this paper will demonstrate how 6F-PAI-2, which does not plasticize up to 850 psia total feed pressure using a 50/50 CO2/ CH4 mixture, becomes signi fi cantly plasticized in the presence of H2S. The purpose of this demonstration is to extend the results of our previous work, which showed the effect of fl uorine concentration on H2S induced plasticization resistance using pure gas testing , to gas mixtures. The remaining bulk of this paper will compare more fl uorinated 6F-PAI-1, due to its better stability against H2S, to Pebax s , a commercially available rubbery polymer. Pebax s is structurally similar to the highly H2S selective polyether/polyamide block copolymer used in the work by Chatterjee et al. [13].

The details of their synthesis are given in our previous works [17,18]. Pebax s SA01 MV 3000, a polyether/polyamide block copolymer, was purchased from Arkema Inc. Technical Polymers. Although the exact chemical structure is proprietary, it is believed to be structurally similar to the polyether/polyamide block copolymer shown in Fig. 1b [14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Dense fi lm membranes were prepared by knife casting the polymer and solvent ' dope ' on a glass plate using a stainless steel doctor blade with approximately 125 -350 μ mclearance. Solutions of 20 -30 wt% polymer were used for both materials. 6F-PAI-1 was cast out of tetrahydrofuran (THF) and annealed in a vacuum oven at 200 1 C for 24 h. 6F-PAI-2 and 3 were cast in dimethylformamide (DMF) due to insolubility in THF. The fi lms were solvent exchanged in methanol followed by annealing in a vacuum oven at 220 1 C for 24 h. Because Pebax s is insoluble in most common solvents at room temperature, the polymer and solvent, N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP), were heated to approximately 150 1 C to form a viscous and homogeneous solution. The solution was immediately drawn onto a room temperature glass plate. The solidi fi ed polymer fi lm was dried at 120 1 C at ambient pressure, followed by drying in a vacuum oven at 110 1 C for 2 days. Less than 1% residual NMP remained in the polymer fi lm after this drying procedure, which was con fi rmed by thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the absence of swelling, permeability in rubbery polymers is constant, and the sorption and diffusion coef fi cients are independent of pressure. For glassy polymers, the existence of molecular scale packing defects, which are ' trapped ' into the polymer matrix over experimental time scales, introduces a concentration or pressure dependence into the sorption and diffusion coef fi cients. The sorption coef fi cient in glassy polymers is usually a decreasing function of penetrant partial pressure due to saturation of the limited number of glassy packing defects, which provides additional sorption sites beyond the more simple densely packed regions. Penetrant sorption into these defects, which are referred to as Langmuir sites, is analogous to sorption in a zeolite cavity. At elevated pressures, these cavities ' fi ll up ' , and additional sorption occurs primarily in the more densely packed Henry's Law type environments, where the polymer chains are assumed to be in packing equilibrium. Penetrant sorption in glassy media can also be reduced by ' competition ' with a second condensable species for the limited number of Langmuir sorption sites. The effect of pressure as well as ' competition ' on the sorption coef fi cient can be described using the dual-mode sorption model as shown in Eq. (2). The fi rst term on the right hand side of Eq. (2) represents Henry's sorption term while the second term quanti fi es sorption into the Langmuir sites. The sorption coef fi cient is given in units of [cc(STP)/(ccpolymer -psia)].

texts.orig: kD [cc(STP)/(ccpolymer -psia)] is Henry's Law parameter, b (psia � 1 ) is the Langmuir af fi nity constant, CH 0 ½ cc ð STP Þ = ccpolymer  is the Langmuir capacity constant, and f is the fugacity of penetrant i in the feed. Using this equation, the effect of additional components on component i 's sorption coef fi cient can be predicted by including their Langmuir af fi nity constants and fugacities in the denominator of Eq. (2). Species sorbed into Langmuir sites are assumed to be partially immobilized relative to the species sorbed into Henry's Law domains, which have greater diffusional mobility. The two populations are, however, assumed to be in local equilibrium. Consequently, the effect of partial pressure on a penetrant's effective diffusivity is opposite to what is observed with the sorption coef fi cient. In glassy polymers, the effective diffusivity tends to increase with partial pressure even in the absence of plasticization as a greater percentage of sorbed gas species sorb into the more mobile Henry's Law type environments. The dual-mode framework for gas sorption and diffusivity in glassy polymers as just described will be used to interpret the ternary mixed gas data for 6F-PAI-1 in the latter portions of this paper.

Consequently, the effect of partial pressure on a penetrant's effective diffusivity is opposite to what is observed with the sorption coef fi cient. In glassy polymers, the effective diffusivity tends to increase with partial pressure even in the absence of plasticization as a greater percentage of sorbed gas species sorb into the more mobile Henry's Law type environments. The dual-mode framework for gas sorption and diffusivity in glassy polymers as just described will be used to interpret the ternary mixed gas data for 6F-PAI-1 in the latter portions of this paper.

texts.orig: Mixed gas permeation measurements were carried out at 35 1 C using a constant volume, variable pressure method described previously [21]. The downstream, or permeate pressure, was maintained at less than 25 Torr. The primary difference between measuring pure and mixed gas permeation is the application of a retentate stream during mixed gas conditions, which was set to be at least 100 times greater than the permeation fl ow rate in order to prevent concentration polarization in the upstream. The composition of the downstream permeation contents was measured using a gas chromatograph (GC) (Varian Inc.). The average of 3 injections at each pressure point in the permeability isotherm was used to measure the permeability at increasing pressure up to approximately 1000 psia. Establishment of an effective steadystate was veri fi ed when the permeability at a given pressure measured 40 min after a previous measurement agreed within 1%. At a given pressure, steady-state was generally reached in 3 h or less. The mixed gas permeability coef fi cient was calculated using Eq. texts.orig: Both materials show high selectivity (40 or greater) up to 600 psia total pressure. 6F-PAI-3 undergoes plasticization around 600 psia, as inferred by the drop in selectivity near this pressure. 6F-PAI-2, on the other hand, shows stable selectivity above 40 up to approximately 750 psia total feed pressure, which is almost 400 psia CO2 partial pressure. As this material is uncrosslinked, such high selectivity under these conditions is impressive, since most uncrosslinked, pure polymers plasticize at even lower pressures. For instance, under the same feed conditions, cellulose acetate (CA) becomes plasticized near 390 psia total feed pressure, and CO2/CH4 selectivity falls below 25 [23,24].

texts.orig: Because 6F-PAI-3 showed lower CO2 plasticization resistance compared to 6F-PAI-2 based on CO2 alone, it was not chosen for ternary mixed gas testing with H2S. Additionally, in our previous paper, 6F-PAI-2 showed higher plasticization resistance than 6F-PAI-1 using pure CO2 [16]. Contrarily, 6F-PAI-2's plasticization resistance against H2S was lower than 6F-PAI-1. This trend is believed to be caused by higher fl uorine content in 6F-PAI-1's backbone, as H2S solubility has been shown to be reduced in fl uorinated media [16,20]. A comparison between 6F-PAI-2's mixed gas separation performance in the presence of H2S, which is shown in Fig. 3, and 6F-PAI-1's shown later in Section 3.2 demonstrates the vastly different separation performances of these two materials containing different amounts of pendent fl uorine along the polymer backbone. The H2S ' rich ' feed, containing 20/20/60H2S/CO2/CH4, was used to test 6F-PAI-2.

texts.orig: As the data in Fig. 3 demonstrate, 6F-PAI-2's mixed gas separation performance is signi fi cantly impacted in an undesirable manner in the presence of H2S. This is signi fi ed by continual increase in both CH4 and H2S permeability throughout the entire pressure range. A serious loss in CO2/CH4 permselectivity of over 50% coincides with the increase in CH4 permeability. Permselectivity for H2S/CH4 decreases to a much lower extent, which is presumably due to the much smaller size difference between H2S and CH4 (3.6 vs. 3.8 Å) compared to CO2 and CH4 (3.2 vs. 3.8 Å). In other words, because plasticization reduces diffusion selectivity, there is less diffusion selectivity to lose for the (H2S/CH4) pair than for the (CO2/ CH4) pair once plasticization occurs. In order to verify that the results shown in Fig. 3 were due to the presence of H2S and not simply an experimental artifact, the membrane was degassed and evacuated for approximately one month while other permeation experiments were run, then reloaded into the permeation cell for additional mixed gas testing. A binary feed containing only CO2/CH4 at a composition of 50/50 was then applied to the upstream and a permeability isotherm was measured in a similar manner as has been used for all mixed gas testing in this work. Fig. 4 shows CO2 and CH4 permeability and CO2/CH4 permselectivity vs. pressure for the ' H2S conditioned ' fi lm.

texts.orig: The effect of varying acid gas concentration on CH4's permeability shown in Fig. 5b is much less pronounced than for CO2, which is typical for non-condensable gases. Methane permeability is, however, reduced in both feeds relative to its pure gas permeability isotherm. Up to approximately 600 psia total feed pressure, CH4 permeability in both feeds is between 0.45 and 0.5 Barrer. These similar permeability values in feeds containing very different acid gas concentration suggests that the speci fi c acid gas composition has only a minor effect on CH4 permeability, and is again presumably a result of CH4's low sorption af fi nity. Based on these results, it appears that the effect of additional, condensable components in the feed on the permeability of both CO2 and CH4 can be rationalized by conventional competitive sorption arguments, which is the most often observed trend in glassy polymers.

texts.orig: Interestingly, the effect of varying acid gas concentration in the feed on H2S permeability in 6F-PAI-1 is opposite to what is shown for CO2 and CH4. That is, H2S permeability in the rich feed is lower than it is in the lean feed. Intuitively, H2S permeability was expected to be lower in the lean feed due to increased competition with a higher concentration of CO2 for Langmuir sorption sites. Plasticization is not believed to cause such higher permeability in the lean feed, as the permeability of all three gases is not an increasing function of pressure between 200 and 400 psia. A possible explanation for this surprising trend may be that in the H2S lean feed, greater suppression of H2S's Langmuir sorption capacity occurs due to higher CO2 concentration in the feed. This may cause a greater fraction of H2S to sorb into the equilibrium packed Henry's environments. Gases sorbed into the Henry's regions have greater diffusional mobility compared to the Langmuir regions. The effective diffusion coef fi cient, which is a function of diffusivity in both Henry's and Langmuir environments, tends to increase with penetrant concentration sorbed in the membrane, because a greater fraction of penetrant will populate the more mobile Henry's Law type sites. Therefore, the effective diffusion coef fi cient for H2S in the lean feed at low total feed

texts.orig: Similar to the CO2/CH4 selectivity trends for 6F-PAI-1 shown in Fig. 6, the H2S/CH4 selectivity in Fig. 7b is higher in the lean feed due to higher H2S permeability in this feed relative to the rich feed. However, in this case, such higher H2S permeability may be due to higher effective H2S diffusivity as opposed to dual-mode competition effects which are presumed to affect the CO2 permeability shown in Fig. 5a. Furthermore, H2S/CH4 selectivity in the lean feed is mostly constant with feed pressure, consistent with the fairly constant H2S and CH4 permeability below 600 psia. In the rich feed, H2S/CH4 selectivity is an increasing function of pressure. This is a surprising result considering that beyond 400 psia, CH4 permeability in the rich feed shown in Fig. 5b also increases, presumably due to plasticization. The increase in H2S/ CH4 selectivity with pressure for the rich feed is likely due to the fact that H2S/CH4 diffusion selectivity in 6F-PAI-1 is close to unity [19]. Selectivity reduction in plasticized membranes usually stems from reduced diffusion selectivity as the polymer chains become more mobile and free volume increases. Because H2S and CH4 have similar diffusivities in 6F-PAI-1, increases in chain mobility will affect both gases to a similar extent. Therefore, the combination of increasing effective diffusivity for H2S at low pressures, as well as increased segmental chain mobility at higher pressure, may cause H2S diffusivity to increase more rapidly than that of CH4. These results further demonstrate the subtle yet important differences between H2S and CO2 transport in glassy polymers. Surprisingly, the H2S/CH4 permselectivity in 6F-PAI-1 does not appear to decrease from plasticization effects as does the CO2/CH4 permselectivity.

texts.orig: In summary, H2S transport in glassy polymers appears to be more dependent on differences in penetrant sorption and diffusion in the glassy, dual mode environments than does CO2. As will be shown in the fi nal section of this paper, the favorable CO2/CH4 permselectivity and moderate H2S/CH4 selectivity in 6F-PAI-1 under these aggressive conditions translates into low CH4 loss in the downstream.

texts.orig: The experimental pure gas sorption and permeation isotherms for Pebax s dense fi lms are displayed in Fig. 8.
texts.text: The experimental pure gas sorption and permeation isotherms for Pebax s dense fi lms are displayed in Fig. 8.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Because rubbery polymers are devoid of ' permanent ' microvoid packing defects, their transport properties are generally constant in the absence of penetrant induced swelling. Such independence on feed pressure is displayed in the sorption coef fi cient isotherms for both CO2 and CH4 displayed in Fig. 8a. The permeability isotherm for CH4 is also constant with feed pressure. Carbon dioxide permeability appears to increase slightly with feed pressure. Because CO2's sorption coef fi cient in Fig. 8a is constant over the same pressure range, the increase in its permeability shown in Fig. 8b suggests an increase in CO2's diffusion coef fi cient. This is presumably caused by localized increases in polymer chain mobility as a result of increased CO2 concentration in the membrane. In contrast to CH4 and CO2, both H2S's sorption and permeability coef fi cients increase with pressure above 100 psia. This indicates that both H2S sorption and diffusion are increasing functions of pressure. These trends in H2S transport demonstrate that rubbery materials, particularly those containing H2S-philic groups such as ether and amide segments in Pebax s , are susceptible to penetrant induced swelling and plasticization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The pure gas permeability, sorption, and diffusion selectivities for Pebax s are given in Table 3. In general, rubbery polymers have very low size discrimination capabilities, which is apparent in Pebax s 's low CO2/CH4 diffusion selectivity. For the H2S/CH4 pair, diffusion selectivity in Pebax s is only slightly above unity. Therefore, permselectivity for this pair is derived almost entirely through H2S's much greater sorption compared to CH4. Even for the CO2/CH4 system, the selective separation of CO2 from CH4 in Pebax s is based mostly on CO2's higher sorption level in the membrane. These results highlight the difference in selectivity mechanisms between rubbery and glassy polymers. In the remaining sections of this paper, we will show that despite the increase in H2S sorption with feed pressure, total H2S/CH4 permselectivity in the ternary mixed gas feeds in Pebax s does not bene fi t from such an increase.

texts.orig: As can be observed in Fig. 10, feed stream H2S concentration relative to CO2 does not appear to have a signi fi cant effect on the permselectivity of CO2/CH4, as selectivity for this gas pair is between 9 and 11 in both feeds. A similar value for the H2S/CH4 selectivity in both rich and lean feeds is also observed at low total feed pressure. It should be pointed out that CO2/CH4 selectivity in Pebax s for both feeds is less than 50% of the value shown for 6F-PAI-1 subjected to the same feed conditions. This is a result of very low diffusion selectivity in Pebax s , which as shown in Table 3, is near unity for H2S /CH4 and only slightly above 2 for CO2/CH4. For the H2S /CH4 mixed gas selectivity, the increase in H2S sorption resulting from membrane swelling, which was shown in the pure gas sorption isotherms, does not appear to affect total permselectivity. Although H2S/CH4 selectivity in Pebax s is more than double that shown for 6F-PAI-1, such higher selectivity does not result in signi fi cantly less CH4 loss in the downstream compared to 6F-PAI-1, which will be discussed in the following section.

texts.text: 4.3. Effect of membrane selectivity and feed stream composition on CH4 loss in the downstream

texts.orig: The mole fractions of CH4 in the permeate stream are plotted against total feed pressure in Fig. 11 for both feed streams and support the trends just discussed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the H2S lean feed, signi fi cantly less CH4 is lost in the permeate stream for 6F-PAI-1 compared to Pebax s under the same conditions. This demonstrates that in conditions where CO2 feed concentration is higher than H2S, CO2/CH4 selectivity may be more important in determining CH4 loss in the downstream compared to H2S/CH4 selectivity. In the rich feed, CH4 permeate composition is similar for both materials at pressure below 600 psia. At higher pressure, CH4 loss in the downstream for Pebax s becomes noticeably greater than for 6F-PAI-1, which is likely due to swelling in Pebax s induced by elevated partial pressures of the acid gases. Because of the similar concentrations of the acid gases in the rich feed, it does not appear that either material has a clear advantage in terms of CH4 loss in the downstream. It does appear, however, that in the more common case where CO2 concentration is higher than H2S, higher CO2/CH4 selectivity shown by glassy 6FPAI-1 may result in less CH4 loss in the downstream. These results suggest that glassy polymers are likely to be more suitable than rubbery materials for separations involving higher CO2 concentration compared to H2S.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we showed that incorporation of fl uorine into a glassy polymer backbone may contribute to plasticization resistance against H2S. Using a ternary mixed gas feed composed of 20/20/60H2S/CO2/CH4, 6F-PAI-2 plasticized at 40 psia H2S partial pressure, which was the lowest feed pressure tested. With increasing feed pressure, selectivity for both gas pairs, CO2/CH4 and H2S/CH4, dropped to below 10. Upon removal of the H2S containing feed and application of a binary feed containing 50/50 CO2/CH4, 6F-PAI-2 returned to its unplasticized state, and maintained a CO2/CH4 selectivity of above 40 at 400 psia CO2 partial pressure. Using the same 20/20/60H2S/CO2/CH4 feed, more fl uorinated 6F-PAI-1 maintained a CO2/CH4 selectivity of above 30 at 160 psia H2S partial pressure or 800 psia total pressure. Although plasticization occurred in 6F -PAI-1 near 80 psia H2S partial pressure, it still showed attractive selectivity for both gas pairs under these industrially relevant and highly aggressive feed conditions. These results suggest that fl uorine may be a useful tool for stabilizing membranes speci fi cally against H2S. Additionally, compared to a highly H2S selective rubbery material, 6F-PAI-1 showed less CH4 loss in the downstream for feeds containing both low and similar concentrations of H2S relative to CO2. These results indicate that the more CO2 selective glassy polymers may be more suitable as membranes in conditions where H2S concentration in the feed is much lower than CO2, which is the case for many conventional natural gas wells. In summary, glassy polymers, speci fi cally highly fl uorinated 6FDA based polyamide-imides, represent a competitive platform of polymer membrane materials for natural gas puri fi cation due to their good intrinsic stability against highly interacting feed components as well as favorable CO2/CH4 selectivity.
